content: There are infinitely many prime numbers — a result proven by Euclid around 300 BCE in his work 'Elements'.
The Indian mathematician Brahmagupta defined arithmetic rules for zero as a number in 628 CE, not merely as a placeholder.
The Fibonacci sequence — where each number is the sum of the two before it — appears in nature, such as the spiral arrangement of sunflower seeds and pinecones.
Kurt Goedel's incompleteness theorems (1931) showed that any consistent formal system strong enough for arithmetic contains true statements it cannot prove.
Light travels at exactly 299,792,458 metres per second in a vacuum. This fixed value is used to define the length of the metre.
Absolute zero, the lowest temperature physically possible, is 0 kelvin — equal to about minus 273.15 degrees Celsius.
Sound cannot travel through the vacuum of space because it needs a physical medium, such as air or water, to propagate.
Diamond and graphite are both made entirely of carbon; they differ only in how their atoms are bonded together.
Helium was first identified in the spectrum of the Sun in 1868 — making it the only element discovered in space before it was found on Earth.
A day on Venus is longer than its year: Venus rotates once every 243 Earth days but completes an orbit of the Sun every 225 Earth days.
Sunlight takes about 8 minutes and 20 seconds to travel the roughly 150 million kilometres from the Sun to Earth.
Saturn's average density is lower than water's, so in principle the planet would float if a body of water large enough to hold it existed.
Neutron stars are so dense that a piece of one the size of a sugar cube would weigh roughly a billion tonnes on Earth.
Octopuses have three hearts and blue blood, because their blood uses copper-based hemocyanin instead of iron-based hemoglobin to carry oxygen.
The blue whale is the largest animal known to have ever lived, reaching lengths of up to about 30 metres.
The adult human brain contains roughly 86 billion neurons, according to careful cell-counting studies.
The human body is made up of an estimated 37 trillion cells.
The deepest known point in the ocean is the Challenger Deep in the Mariana Trench, at about 10,935 metres below sea level.
More than 80 percent of Earth's ocean remains unmapped, unobserved, and unexplored.
Mount Everest's summit is the highest point above sea level on Earth, measured in 2020 at 8,848.86 metres.
Water is unusual among common substances because it expands when it freezes, which is why ice floats on liquid water.
Russia is the largest country in the world by land area and spans 11 time zones.
Vatican City is the smallest sovereign state in the world, covering only about 0.44 square kilometres.
Mandarin Chinese has more native speakers than any other language, with roughly 900 million people speaking it as a first language.
The word 'alphabet' comes from 'alpha' and 'beta', the first two letters of the Greek alphabet.
The Great Pyramid of Giza was the tallest human-made structure in the world for over 3,800 years.
The Roman numeral system has no symbol for zero.
Ada Lovelace is widely regarded as the first computer programmer for the algorithm she wrote in the 1840s for Charles Babbage's proposed Analytical Engine.
Adults have 206 bones, while a newborn baby starts with about 300 bones that gradually fuse together during growth.
Pure honey can remain edible for thousands of years; archaeologists have found preserved honey in ancient Egyptian tombs.
The golden ratio, approximately 1.618, is a proportion that has long been associated with balanced, aesthetically pleasing design in art and architecture.
The Sahara is the world's largest hot desert, with an area comparable to that of the entire United States or China.
The temperature at Earth's inner core is estimated to be around 5,200 degrees Celsius — comparable to the surface of the Sun.
Pi is an irrational number: its decimal expansion never ends and never settles into a repeating pattern.
A googol is the number 1 followed by 100 zeros. The search engine Google took its name from a misspelling of it.
The Four Colour Theorem proves any flat map can be coloured with just four colours so that no two adjacent regions share a colour.
A lightning bolt can heat the surrounding air to about 30,000 kelvin — roughly five times hotter than the surface of the Sun.
Sound travels about four times faster in water than in air, because water is denser and less compressible.
Earth's atmosphere is roughly 78% nitrogen and 21% oxygen by volume; the remaining 1% is argon, carbon dioxide and trace gases.
Tungsten has the highest melting point of any metal, about 3,422 degrees Celsius.
Glass is an amorphous solid, not a slow-flowing liquid; uneven old window panes come from how they were made, not from flowing over time.
Sharks are older than trees: sharks have existed for more than 400 million years, predating the earliest trees.
Botanically, bananas are berries, while strawberries are not — a berry develops from a single flower with one ovary.
Honeybees tell hivemates the direction and distance to food through a movement known as the waggle dance.
Tardigrades, tiny animals also called water bears, can survive extreme cold, intense radiation, and even the vacuum of space.
Antarctica is the largest desert on Earth — a desert is defined by very low precipitation, not by heat.
About 97% of Earth's water is salt water in the oceans; only a small fraction is fresh water available for drinking.
The Atlantic Ocean widens by roughly 2 to 3 centimetres per year as new seafloor forms at the Mid-Atlantic Ridge.
Canada has the longest coastline of any country in the world, at over 200,000 kilometres.
The United Nations has 193 member states.
The Pacific Ocean covers a greater area than all of Earth's land combined.
Papua New Guinea has more living languages than any other country — well over 800.
Counting both native and second-language speakers, English is the most widely spoken language in the world.
Cleopatra lived closer in time to the first Moon landing than to the building of the Great Pyramid of Giza.
The Great Wall of China is not a single continuous wall but many walls built and rebuilt across different dynasties.
The term 'computer bug' spread after a moth was found in a Harvard Mark II relay in 1947 and taped into the logbook.
Ray Tomlinson chose the @ symbol to separate the user from the host in email addresses in 1971.
The Eiffel Tower can grow more than 15 centimetres taller in summer as its iron structure expands in the heat.
The cornea of the eye has no blood vessels; it gets most of its oxygen directly from the air.
The Ship of Theseus asks whether an object whose parts have all been replaced over time remains the same object.
There are more possible games of chess than there are atoms in the observable universe.
Zero is an even number, because it is an integer multiple of two.
On a flat surface, the three interior angles of any triangle always add up to 180 degrees.
Hot water can sometimes freeze faster than cold water — a debated phenomenon named the Mpemba effect after the student who observed it.
The Sun accounts for about 99.8% of all the mass in the Solar System.
Jupiter is the largest planet in the Solar System — more than 1,300 Earths could fit inside it.
A rainbow is really a full circle of light; from the ground we usually see only the upper arc.
Mercury is the closest planet to the Sun, but Venus is the hottest because of its dense, heat-trapping atmosphere.
An adult human body is roughly 60% water by weight.
The largest known living organism is a honey fungus in Oregon that spreads across several square kilometres of forest soil.
Starfish have no brain and no blood; they move and feed using a water-powered network of tube feet.
Penguins live almost entirely in the Southern Hemisphere.
The Dead Sea is so salty that swimmers float on its surface with ease.
Because Earth bulges at the equator, the summit of Ecuador's Mount Chimborazo is the point on the surface farthest from Earth's centre — not Everest.
Natural diamonds form deep underground under intense heat and pressure over billions of years.
Australia is both a country and a continent.
Istanbul straddles two continents, with neighbourhoods in both Europe and Asia.
The word 'robot' comes from the Czech 'robota', meaning forced labour; it was coined in Karel Capek's 1920 play R.U.R.
Sign languages are complete natural languages with their own grammar, not simply hand signs for spoken words.
Teaching at the University of Oxford began before the Aztec Empire was founded.
The shortest recorded war, the Anglo-Zanzibar War of 1896, lasted under an hour.
The first message sent over ARPANET in 1969 was meant to be 'LOGIN', but the system crashed after the first two letters, 'LO'.
Leonardo da Vinci's Mona Lisa has no clearly visible eyebrows.
The three smallest bones in the human body — the malleus, incus, and stapes — are found in the middle ear.
Bananas are faintly radioactive because they contain naturally occurring potassium-40.
Earth's days are slowly getting longer as the Moon gradually drifts farther away.
A leap year adds an extra day almost every four years to keep the calendar aligned with Earth's orbit around the Sun.
The equals sign (=) was introduced in 1557 by the Welsh mathematician Robert Recorde.
A dozen is 12 and a gross is a dozen dozen, equal to 144.
Helium balloons float because helium is lighter than the surrounding air.
Light travels so fast it could circle the Earth about seven and a half times in a single second.
Rust forms when iron reacts with oxygen and water to produce iron oxide.
Stars appear to twinkle because their light is bent as it passes through Earth's moving atmosphere.
A year on Mercury — one orbit of the Sun — lasts only about 88 Earth days.
Humans are the only animals known to blush.
A hummingbird's heart can beat more than 1,200 times per minute during flight.
Trees can share nutrients and warning signals through underground networks of fungi linking their roots.
The axolotl, a Mexican salamander, can regrow lost limbs, parts of its heart, and even portions of its brain.
Ants have no lungs; they take in oxygen through tiny holes called spiracles along their bodies.
Contrary to the saying, lightning can and often does strike the same place many times.
Earth is the only planet in the Solar System not named after a Greek or Roman god.
Volcanic lightning can flash within the dense ash clouds of an erupting volcano.
Africa is the only continent that lies in all four hemispheres — northern, southern, eastern, and western.
The Great Barrier Reef is the largest living structure on Earth and can be seen from space.
Monaco is the second-smallest country in the world, after Vatican City.
Esperanto is a constructed language created in the late 1880s to be easy to learn as a neutral international tongue.
The Hawaiian alphabet uses only 13 letters.
The first modern Olympic Games were held in Athens, Greece, in 1896.
Vikings reached North America around the year 1000, roughly 500 years before Christopher Columbus.
The first webcam was set up at the University of Cambridge to watch a coffee pot so people could avoid empty-pot trips.
The QWERTY keyboard layout dates from the 1870s and was designed for early mechanical typewriters.
Wolfgang Amadeus Mozart began composing music at around the age of five.
The human body produces roughly 1 to 1.5 litres of saliva every day.
Socrates wrote nothing himself; most of what we know about him comes from his student Plato.
The dot over a lowercase letter 'i' or 'j' is called a tittle.
A group of crows is traditionally called a murder.
The trolley problem is a thought experiment asking whether one should divert a runaway trolley to save several lives at the cost of one.
Occam's razor is the principle that, all else being equal, the simplest explanation is usually the best.
Rene Descartes argued 'I think, therefore I am': the very act of doubting proves that a thinker exists.
A syllogism is a logical argument with two premises and a conclusion, a form first analysed systematically by Aristotle.
Utilitarianism judges actions by whether they produce the greatest good for the greatest number of people.
The Great Pyramid of Giza was originally clad in smooth white limestone that made it gleam in the sunlight.
Gothic cathedrals use pointed arches and flying buttresses to support tall walls and large stained-glass windows.
On a colour wheel, hues placed directly opposite one another are called complementary colours.
In Western music, moving up one octave doubles the frequency of a note.
The Taj Mahal in Agra, India, was built in the 17th century as a mausoleum for the wife of emperor Shah Jahan.
Impressionist painters such as Claude Monet sought to capture the changing effects of light rather than precise detail.
Antibiotics fight bacterial infections but have no effect on viruses such as those causing the common cold.
The liver is the only internal human organ that can regrow lost tissue.
Vaccines train the immune system to recognise a pathogen without causing the disease itself.
An adult human heart beats roughly 100,000 times a day.
An algorithm is a precise step-by-step procedure for solving a problem or completing a task.
Binary code represents all data using only two digits, 0 and 1.
The World Wide Web was invented by Tim Berners-Lee, who wrote his proposal for it in 1989.
GPS works by using the timing signals from several orbiting satellites to pinpoint a receiver's location.
Onomatopoeia describes words that imitate the sounds they refer to, such as 'buzz' or 'splash'.
A lingua franca is a shared language used for communication between speakers of different native languages.
The International Phonetic Alphabet provides a distinct symbol for each recognised speech sound.
A prime number has exactly two distinct positive divisors: 1 and the number itself.
Probability is measured on a scale from 0, meaning impossible, to 1, meaning certain.
A circle's circumference divided by its diameter always equals pi, approximately 3.14159.
The factorial of a number is the product of all positive integers up to it; for example, 4 factorial equals 24.
Water boils at 100 degrees Celsius at sea level, but at lower temperatures higher up, where air pressure is reduced.
Iron is the most abundant element on Earth by mass, largely because it makes up much of the planet's core.
Every magnet has two poles, north and south; cutting one in half simply creates two smaller magnets.
Photosynthesis is the process by which plants use sunlight to turn carbon dioxide and water into energy and oxygen.
DNA stores genetic instructions in a twisted double-helix structure built from four chemical bases.
Jellyfish have drifted through the oceans for more than 500 million years, long before the dinosaurs.
Earth's magnetic field shields the planet from much of the Sun's harmful stream of charged particles.
A single handful of healthy soil can contain billions of microorganisms.
The world is divided into 24 standard time zones, each spanning roughly 15 degrees of longitude.
The Equator passes through 13 countries across South America, Africa, and Asia.
Johannes Gutenberg's printing press, developed around 1440, made books far cheaper and helped spread literacy.
The ancient Romans built with a form of concrete so durable that many of their structures still stand today.
Latin is the ancestor of the Romance languages, which include Spanish, French, Italian, Portuguese, and Romanian.
The imaginary unit 'i' is defined as the square root of negative one, extending arithmetic to complex numbers.
Roman numerals use the letters I, V, X, L, C, D, and M to represent numbers.
Light from distant stars can take thousands or millions of years to reach us, so we see them as they were long ago.
Electric current is the flow of electric charge, usually carried by electrons moving through a wire.
Sound is a vibration that travels as a wave through a medium such as air, water, or solid material.
The human body is home to trillions of bacteria, most of which are harmless or actively helpful to health.
Chameleons change colour mainly to regulate temperature and communicate, not only to blend in with their surroundings.
A newborn kangaroo is only about the size of a jellybean when it is born.
Most earthquakes happen along the boundaries where Earth's tectonic plates meet.
The ozone layer high in the atmosphere absorbs most of the Sun's harmful ultraviolet radiation.
Indonesia is made up of more than 17,000 islands.
At their closest point in the Bering Strait, Russia and the United States lie less than 4 kilometres apart.
The ancient Library of Alexandria was one of the largest and most important collections of knowledge in the ancient world.
A pixel is the smallest controllable element of a digital image on a screen.
Pottery is one of the oldest human crafts, with examples dating back tens of thousands of years.
A fever is part of the body's immune response and helps it fight off infection.
Hiccups are caused by sudden involuntary contractions of the diaphragm, the muscle used in breathing.
Blood is red because of haemoglobin, an iron-rich protein that carries oxygen around the body.
RAM, a computer's random-access memory, loses its contents when the machine is switched off.
A byte is made up of 8 bits.
Bluetooth wireless technology is named after Harald Bluetooth, a 10th-century Scandinavian king who united tribes.
The most common letter in everyday English text is 'e'.
A pangram is a sentence that uses every letter of the alphabet, such as 'The quick brown fox jumps over the lazy dog'.
Braille represents letters and numbers using patterns of raised dots that are read by touch.
Aristotle was a student of Plato, who in turn was a student of Socrates.
Stoicism teaches focusing on what is within one's control and calmly accepting what is not.
Zeno's paradoxes are famous arguments that appear to show, puzzlingly, that motion is impossible.
An even number is divisible by 2 with no remainder; an odd number is not.
The Pythagorean theorem states that in a right triangle, the square of the longest side equals the sum of the squares of the other two.
A percentage is simply a fraction expressed out of 100.
Diamond is the hardest known naturally occurring material.
Evaporation is the process by which a liquid gradually turns into a gas.
The three states of matter most familiar on Earth are solid, liquid, and gas.
A caterpillar transforms into a butterfly through a process called metamorphosis.
Coral reefs are built over time by tiny animals called coral polyps.
Most of a tree's mass comes from carbon dioxide drawn out of the air, not from the soil.
A geyser is a hot spring that periodically erupts, throwing water and steam into the air.
Sand dunes can slowly migrate over time as wind carries their grains from one side to the other.
Lake Baikal in Russia is the deepest freshwater lake in the world.
Vatican City issues its own postage stamps and mints its own euro coins.
The wheel was invented thousands of years ago; some of the earliest known wheels were used for making pottery before transport.
Ancient Egyptian hieroglyphs were finally deciphered with the help of the Rosetta Stone.
A fresco is a painting made on wet plaster, so the colours become part of the wall as it dries.
Helium can make a person's voice sound higher because sound travels faster through it than through ordinary air.
A 'baker's dozen' is 13 — one more than a standard dozen.
The '#' symbol is sometimes called an octothorpe.
The small plastic or metal tip at the end of a shoelace is called an aglet.
The infinity symbol, written as a sideways figure eight, is called a lemniscate.
A 'jiffy' is an actual unit of time used in physics, though its exact length depends on the field.
Epistemology is the branch of philosophy concerned with the nature, sources, and limits of knowledge.
Existentialism emphasises individual freedom and the responsibility each person has to create their own meaning.
The 'golden rule' — treat others as you would like to be treated — appears across many cultures and traditions.
The pupil of the eye widens in dim light to let in more light and narrows in bright light.
The body can produce vitamin D when the skin is exposed to sunlight.
Warming up before exercise gradually raises the heart rate and prepares the muscles for activity.
The colours of the visible spectrum are often remembered by the name ROYGBIV: red, orange, yellow, green, blue, indigo, violet.
A symphony is usually a large work for orchestra, typically written in several movements.
Origami is the Japanese art of folding paper into decorative or representational shapes.
Zero factorial (0!) is defined to equal 1.
A right angle measures exactly 90 degrees.
A polygon is named for its number of sides: a pentagon has five, a hexagon six, and an octagon eight.
Static electricity can build up when two materials rub together and transfer electric charge.
A glass prism can split white light into the colours of the rainbow by bending each colour by a different amount.
Whales are mammals, not fish: they breathe air and nurse their young.
Spiders are not insects: they have eight legs and two body segments, while insects have six legs and three.
Stalactites hang down from cave ceilings, while stalagmites build up from the cave floor.
The water cycle continually moves water between the oceans, the atmosphere, and the land through evaporation and precipitation.
Open-source software makes its source code freely available for anyone to study, use, and modify.
The Prime Meridian, the line of 0 degrees longitude, passes through Greenwich in London.
A palindrome reads the same forwards and backwards, like the word 'level' or 'racecar'.
The ancient Olympic Games were held every four years, and that four-year span was called an Olympiad.
Paper money was first used in China, centuries before it came into use in Europe.
The Code of Hammurabi, from ancient Babylon, is one of the earliest known written sets of laws.
The Black Death, a plague pandemic, killed a large fraction of Europe's population in the 14th century.
The Renaissance was a period of renewed interest in art and learning that began in Italy.
The Roman town of Pompeii was buried and preserved under volcanic ash when Mount Vesuvius erupted in 79 CE.
A group of lions is called a pride.
Bubble wrap was originally invented as a textured wallpaper before it became packaging material.
Honeybees must visit a very large number of flowers to produce even a single jar of honey.
Mount Everest sits on the border between Nepal and China.
Greenland is the world's largest island that is not also a continent.
The Grand Canyon was carved over millions of years, mainly by the flow of the Colorado River.
Tsunamis are large ocean waves most often triggered by underwater earthquakes.
A cognate is a word that shares a common origin with a word in another language, like English 'night' and German 'Nacht'.
Etymology is the study of the origin and history of words.
A QR code can store much more information than a traditional one-dimensional barcode.
In computing, 'the cloud' refers to servers and services accessed over the internet rather than from a local machine.
In a fraction, the top number is the numerator and the bottom number is the denominator.
Prime factorisation breaks a whole number down into the prime numbers that multiply together to make it.
Friction is the force that resists motion when two surfaces rub against each other.
Ignoring air resistance, gravity makes all objects fall toward Earth at the same rate regardless of their mass.
Pollinators such as bees and butterflies help many plants reproduce by carrying pollen between flowers.
Regular handwashing is one of the most effective ways to prevent the spread of many illnesses.
Perspective is a drawing technique that creates the illusion of depth and distance on a flat surface.
Logic is the study of correct reasoning and what makes an argument valid.
Atoms are mostly empty space, with nearly all their mass packed into a tiny central nucleus.
Helium is the second most abundant element in the universe, after hydrogen.
Temperature is a measure of the average kinetic energy of the particles in a substance.
Antibodies are proteins the immune system makes to recognise and help neutralise specific invaders.
A species is generally defined as a group of organisms that can interbreed and produce fertile offspring.
Migration is the regular, often seasonal, movement of animals from one region to another.
A leap second is occasionally added to official clocks to keep them in step with Earth's slightly irregular rotation.
Clouds form when water vapour in the air cools and condenses into tiny droplets or ice crystals.
Parallel lines are lines in a plane that never meet, no matter how far they are extended.
The mean, or average, of a set of numbers is their sum divided by how many numbers there are.
Every prime number greater than 2 is odd, because any even number is divisible by 2.
Brazil is the largest country in South America by both area and population.
The United Nations has six official languages: Arabic, Chinese, English, French, Russian, and Spanish.
The Magna Carta, agreed in 1215, limited the power of the English king and influenced later ideas about rights.
The first sustained, powered, controlled airplane flight was made by the Wright brothers in 1903.
A firewall is a security system that monitors and controls the traffic flowing in and out of a network.
Many touchscreens locate a finger by sensing tiny changes in electrical charge where it touches the display.
An idiom is a phrase whose meaning cannot be worked out from its individual words, like 'break a leg'.
Most writing systems are read left to right, but Arabic and Hebrew are read right to left.
Sleep helps the brain consolidate memories and gives the body time to repair itself.
The skin is the largest organ of the human body.
Sculpture can be made by carving material away, as in stone, or by building it up, as with clay.
A paradox is a statement that seems to contradict itself, yet may point to a deeper truth.
The Eiffel Tower was originally intended to be a temporary structure for a world's fair.
A light-year measures distance, not time: it is how far light travels in one year.
A magnet's pull is strongest at its poles.
Combining all the colours of light together produces white light.
Nocturnal animals are active mainly at night and rest during the day.
Some lizards can shed and later regrow their tails to escape predators.
The cells of the human body are continually replaced as old ones die and new ones are produced.
The strength of an earthquake is reported using scales such as the moment magnitude scale.
Most of Earth's fresh water is locked away frozen in ice caps and glaciers.
A shape has symmetry if it looks the same after being flipped, turned, or slid.
A square is a special rectangle whose four sides are all equal in length.
Negative numbers are less than zero and are written with a minus sign in front.
The Amazon River carries more water than any other river in the world.
The Tokyo metropolitan area is one of the most populous urban regions on Earth.
The Great Fire of London in 1666 destroyed much of the medieval centre of the city.
The ancient Maya developed a sophisticated calendar system and their own writing.
GPS, Wi-Fi, and Bluetooth all use radio waves to send information without wires.
A web cookie is a small file a website stores on your device to remember information between visits.
Sign language users convey tone and emphasis through facial expressions and movement, not just hand shapes.
Tonal languages, such as Mandarin Chinese, use changes in pitch to distinguish the meanings of words.
Drinking enough water helps the body regulate its temperature and carry nutrients to cells.
The heart pumps blood through a network of arteries, veins, and tiny capillaries.
Photography captures an image by letting light fall onto a light-sensitive surface or electronic sensor.
Ethics is the branch of philosophy that examines what is morally right and wrong.
Antarctica has no permanent human residents — only researchers and support staff who stay temporarily.
The metric system of measurement is used by the great majority of countries in the world.
A decade is ten years, a century is one hundred years, and a millennium is one thousand years.
An echo is simply sound reflecting off a distant surface and returning to the listener.
Solar panels convert sunlight directly into electricity.
Density describes how much mass is packed into a given volume of a substance.
Frogs begin life as tadpoles that breathe with gills before developing lungs and legs.
Bats are the only mammals capable of true sustained flight.
Rivers carry sediment that can build up into fertile deltas where they reach the sea.
Earth's seasons are caused by the tilt of its axis as the planet orbits the Sun.
The perimeter of a shape is the total distance around its outer edges.
Multiplication can be seen as repeated addition: 3 times 4 is the same as 4 plus 4 plus 4.
The area of a rectangle is found by multiplying its length by its width.
Switzerland is well known for its long-standing policy of neutrality in international conflicts.
The Nile flows northward through northeastern Africa.
The Industrial Revolution, beginning in the 18th century, transformed manufacturing through the use of machines.
The ancient Silk Road was a network of trade routes that connected Asia with Europe.
A robot is a machine that can be programmed to carry out tasks automatically.
Solar-powered calculators can run on light alone, with no batteries required.
A dialect is a regional or social variety of a language with its own words and pronunciations.
Punctuation marks help show the structure and intended meaning of written sentences.
Teeth are coated in enamel, the hardest substance in the human body.
Regular exercise strengthens the heart and improves the body's blood circulation.
A mural is a large painting made directly onto a wall or ceiling.
Aesthetics is the branch of philosophy concerned with beauty, art, and taste.
The Statue of Liberty was a gift from the people of France to the United States.
A rainbow always appears in the part of the sky opposite the Sun.
Mixing an acid with a base can neutralise both, often producing water and a salt.
Euler's identity, e raised to the i-pi power plus 1 equals 0, links five of the most important constants in mathematics.
A function is continuous if arbitrarily small changes in its input produce small changes in its output.
In abstract algebra, a group is a set with an operation satisfying closure, associativity, an identity element, and inverses.
The derivative of a function measures its instantaneous rate of change.
A matrix is a rectangular array of numbers often used to represent linear transformations.
Probability theory provides the mathematical foundation for statistics and the analysis of risk.
Einstein's general theory of relativity describes gravity as the curving of spacetime by mass and energy.
The Heisenberg uncertainty principle says a particle's position and momentum cannot both be known with perfect precision.
Entropy is a measure of disorder, or of the energy in a system that is unavailable to do useful work.
Superconductors can carry electric current with zero resistance once cooled below a critical temperature.
Antimatter is made of particles with the same mass as ordinary matter but opposite electric charge.
The Doppler effect is the change in a wave's observed frequency when its source and the observer move relative to each other.
Nuclear fusion powers the Sun by combining hydrogen nuclei into helium and releasing enormous energy.
A black hole's gravity is so strong that not even light can escape from beyond its event horizon.
The periodic table arranges the chemical elements by atomic number and groups those with similar properties.
Mitochondria, often called the powerhouse of the cell, generate most of a cell's usable chemical energy.
Natural selection is the process by which traits that improve survival and reproduction become more common over generations.
CRISPR is a tool that allows scientists to edit DNA at precise, targeted locations.
Enzymes are biological catalysts that speed up chemical reactions in living organisms.
Plate tectonics explains how Earth's rigid outer shell is broken into large plates that slowly move.
Public-key cryptography uses a pair of keys — one to encrypt a message and a different one to decrypt it.
Machine learning lets computer systems improve at a task by finding patterns in data rather than being explicitly programmed.
Formal logic uses symbols to represent the structure of arguments so their validity can be analysed precisely.
The mind-body problem asks how mental states relate to the physical processes of the brain.
Syntax is the set of rules that governs how words combine into phrases and sentences.
Radioactive elements decay over time, and each has a characteristic half-life — the time for half of a sample to decay.
A laser produces a narrow, focused beam of light of a single colour, with its waves marching in step.
The law of conservation of energy states that energy cannot be created or destroyed, only changed from one form to another.
Sound travels through air at about 343 metres per second at room temperature.
Viruses are not considered fully alive because they cannot reproduce without hijacking a host cell.
Plants and animals exchange gases in a cycle: plants release oxygen and absorb carbon dioxide, while animals do the reverse.
Genes are segments of DNA that carry the instructions for building proteins.
Fossils are the preserved remains or traces of living things from the distant past.
The Richter scale was an early method for measuring the strength of earthquakes.
Geometry is the branch of mathematics concerned with shapes, sizes, positions, and the properties of space.
The order of operations tells you which parts of a mathematical expression to calculate first.
Statistics is the study of collecting, organising, analysing, and interpreting data.
Democracy is a system of government in which power ultimately rests with the people, usually through voting.
A peninsula is a piece of land nearly surrounded by water but still joined to a larger landmass.
The Cold War was a decades-long period of political and military tension between the United States and the Soviet Union.
The ancient Egyptians preserved bodies as mummies as part of their beliefs about the afterlife.
An operating system manages a computer's hardware and provides a platform for running its programs.
3D printing builds physical objects layer by layer from a digital design.
Morphology is the study of how words are built from smaller meaningful units called morphemes.
Loanwords are words borrowed from one language into another, like English 'sushi' from Japanese.
Antiseptics are substances used to kill or slow the growth of germs on living tissue, such as skin.
The nervous system carries electrical and chemical signals between the brain and the rest of the body.
Architecture combines art and engineering to design buildings and other structures.
Metaphysics is the branch of philosophy that studies the fundamental nature of reality and existence.
A magnetic compass needle aligns itself with Earth's magnetic field to point roughly toward the poles.
Surface tension makes the surface of water behave like a thin elastic skin, letting some insects walk on it.
Refraction is the bending of light as it passes from one transparent material into another.
A vacuum is a region of space containing little or no matter.
Corals, sea anemones, and jellyfish all belong to a group of animals called cnidarians.
Some plants are pollinated by the wind, which carries their pollen from flower to flower without animals.
The liver is the largest internal organ in the human body.
Glaciers are slow-moving masses of ice that carve and reshape the land as they flow.
Earth's atmosphere has several layers; the lowest one, where weather occurs and we live, is the troposphere.
An integer is a whole number that may be positive, negative, or zero.
A circle has no corners, and every point on it lies the same distance from its centre.
Exponents show how many times a number is multiplied by itself, so 2 cubed means 2 times 2 times 2.
An archipelago is a group or chain of islands.
A constitution is a set of fundamental principles and rules by which a country is governed.
The Berlin Wall, which divided the city for nearly three decades, fell in 1989.
Writing was invented independently in several ancient civilisations, including Mesopotamia.
The efficiency of an algorithm is often described by how its running time grows as the size of the input increases.
Satellites in orbit relay signals used for television, weather forecasting, and navigation.
Phonetics is the study of the physical sounds of human speech — how they are made, transmitted, and heard.
Semantics is the study of meaning in language.
Red blood cells carry oxygen around the body, while white blood cells help fight infection.
The lungs exchange oxygen and carbon dioxide between the air we breathe and the bloodstream.
Calligraphy is the art of beautiful, decorative handwriting.
Rationalism is the view that reason is a primary source and test of knowledge.
Thousands of years ago, the Sahara was a much greener, wetter landscape with lakes and grassland.
The seven continents are Africa, Antarctica, Asia, Australia, Europe, North America, and South America.
Inertia is the tendency of an object to resist changes to its state of motion.
Convection transfers heat through the movement of a fluid such as air or water.
A catalyst speeds up a chemical reaction without being consumed by it.
Camouflage helps animals blend into their surroundings to avoid being seen by predators or prey.
Hibernation lets some animals survive winter by greatly slowing their heartbeat, breathing, and body temperature.
Chlorophyll is the green pigment plants use to capture light energy for photosynthesis.
Erosion is the gradual wearing away of rock and soil by wind, water, or ice.
The eye of a hurricane is a relatively calm centre surrounded by a ring of powerful winds.
A variable is a symbol, often a letter, that stands for an unknown or changeable number.
The circumference of a circle is the distance all the way around its edge.
Rounding replaces a number with a nearby, simpler value, such as rounding 47 to 50.
An isthmus is a narrow strip of land that connects two larger land areas.
A capital city is the place where a country's or region's government is based.
At its height, the Roman Empire surrounded the entire Mediterranean Sea.
Gunpowder was invented in ancient China, originally during the search for an elixir of life.
A semiconductor is a material whose ability to conduct electricity can be controlled, making modern electronics possible.
Email lets people send written messages over the internet almost instantly.
Grammar is the system of rules that structures how a language is put together.
A synonym is a word that has the same or a very similar meaning to another word.
Bones store minerals such as calcium, and their inner marrow produces blood cells.
A balanced diet includes a variety of foods that together provide the nutrients the body needs.
A still life is an artwork depicting inanimate objects, such as fruit, flowers, or everyday items.
Free will is the philosophical question of whether and how people can genuinely choose their own actions.
A fortnight is a period of two weeks, or fourteen days.
The four cardinal directions are north, south, east, and west.
A fractal is a shape that looks similar at every scale, like a coastline, a fern, or a snowflake.
Modular arithmetic deals with numbers that wrap around upon reaching a set value, the way hours wrap around on a clock.
The number e, approximately 2.718, is the base of the natural logarithm.
Plasma, often called the fourth state of matter, is an ionised gas found in stars and lightning.
Quarks are the elementary particles that combine to form protons and neutrons.
Absolute zero can be approached ever more closely but never actually reached.
Ribosomes are the cellular machines that assemble proteins from amino acids.
Symbiosis is a close, long-term biological interaction between two different species.
DNA and RNA are the two kinds of nucleic acid found in living cells.
Earth's mantle is a thick layer of hot, slowly flowing rock between the thin crust and the core.
Cave stalactites and stalagmites grow extremely slowly, often less than a millimetre per year.
A federation is a system in which power is shared between a central government and regional states.
The Strait of Gibraltar separates Europe from Africa and links the Atlantic Ocean to the Mediterranean Sea.
Cuneiform, an early writing system from Mesopotamia, was pressed into clay with a reed stylus.
The astrolabe was an ancient instrument used to measure the positions of stars and to tell time.
Data compression reduces a file's size by removing redundancy in how its information is stored.
A database organises information so it can be stored, searched, and retrieved efficiently.
Pragmatics studies how context shapes the meaning of what people say.
A phoneme is the smallest unit of sound that can change the meaning of a word.
Insulin is a hormone that helps the body regulate the level of sugar in the blood.
The retina at the back of the eye converts incoming light into nerve signals for the brain.
In music, harmony is the sound of two or more notes played at the same time.
A sculpture 'in the round' can be viewed from every side, unlike a relief carved into a flat surface.
Empiricism is the view that knowledge comes primarily from sensory experience.
On the Celsius scale, water freezes at 0 degrees and boils at 100 degrees at sea level.
In mathematics, a set is simply a collection of distinct objects.
Trigonometry studies the relationships between the angles and the sides of triangles.
A vector has both magnitude and direction, while a scalar has only magnitude.
Momentum is the product of an object's mass and its velocity.
Isotopes are atoms of the same element that have different numbers of neutrons.
The electromagnetic spectrum ranges from low-energy radio waves to high-energy gamma rays.
Osmosis is the movement of water across a membrane toward the side with more dissolved substances.
An ecosystem is all the living things in an area together with the non-living environment they interact with.
Hay fever is an allergic immune reaction to airborne pollen from plants.
A geological fault is a fracture in rock where the two sides have moved relative to one another.
Sedimentary rock forms from layers of sediment that are compacted and cemented over long periods.
A census is an official count of a country's population.
The Suez Canal links the Mediterranean Sea to the Red Sea, greatly shortening sea routes between Europe and Asia.
The ancient city of Petra was carved directly into rose-coloured sandstone cliffs.
Leonardo da Vinci often wrote his notebooks in mirror script, reading right to left.
An API is a set of rules that lets different software programs communicate with one another.
A pixel's colour is usually produced by mixing red, green, and blue light in different amounts.
In a dialect continuum, neighbouring varieties are mutually understandable but distant ones may not be.
Vowels are speech sounds produced without blocking the flow of air through the mouth.
Cartilage is a flexible connective tissue found in joints, the nose, and the ears.
Reflexes are automatic responses that happen without conscious thought, such as pulling away from heat.
Contrast in design uses differences in colour, size, or shape to draw the eye and create emphasis.
A cappella means singing without any instrumental accompaniment.
Deductive reasoning reaches conclusions that must be true if its premises are true.
There are 60 seconds in a minute and 60 minutes in an hour — a base-60 system inherited from ancient Babylon.
A coordinate system uses numbers to pinpoint a location, the way latitude and longitude locate a place on Earth.
Calculus has two main branches: differential calculus and integral calculus.
A theorem is a statement that has been proven true from accepted assumptions.
Velocity describes both how fast something moves and the direction in which it moves.
The pH scale measures how acidic or basic a solution is, running from 0 to 14.
A wavelength is the distance between two successive crests of a wave.
Bacteria reproduce by splitting in two, a process called binary fission.
A food chain describes who eats whom, passing energy from one organism to the next.
Plants spread and reproduce through pollination and through the dispersal of their seeds.
A watershed is the area of land that drains rain and snowmelt into a common river, lake, or sea.
Magma is molten rock beneath Earth's surface; once it erupts onto the surface it is called lava.
Lines of longitude run north to south, while lines of latitude run east to west.
The Panama Canal connects the Atlantic and Pacific Oceans across Central America.
The ancient Romans built a vast network of paved roads, many marked along the way by stone milestones.
The Antikythera mechanism, an ancient Greek device, is considered one of the earliest known analogue computers.
The CPU, or central processing unit, carries out the instructions of a computer program.
A gigabyte is roughly a billion bytes of data.
A suffix is added to the end of a word to change its meaning or grammatical role, like '-ly' in 'quickly'.
Body language and gestures are forms of non-verbal communication.
The kidneys filter waste from the blood and produce urine.
Skeletal muscles often work in pairs: as one contracts, its partner relaxes.
Texture in art refers to how a surface looks or feels — smooth, rough, soft, or patterned.
A portrait is an artwork that depicts a specific person.
Inductive reasoning draws general conclusions from specific observations, though the conclusions are not guaranteed.
There are seven days in a week, a division used by many cultures for thousands of years.
